name: Loot Table
description: A weighted reward definition that selects items, currency, upgrades, or resources from eligible entries.
design_notes: Loot tables are economy tools as much as randomization tools. A useful table defines eligibility, weights, rarity, pity rules, duplicate handling, and progression constraints. The main design risk is not randomness itself, but reward output that breaks pacing, trivializes crafting, or makes players distrust the system.
design_purpose: Turn encounters, chests, rooms, or runs into varied reward outcomes.
Control scarcity and progression through weighted entries and gating rules.
Support replayability while preserving a predictable long-term economy.
player_fantasy: The player feels that rewards can surprise them while still belonging to a fair and understandable economy.

parameters.name: weighted_entry_count
parameters.description: Number of selectable entries in the table.

parameters.name: rarity_tier_weights
parameters.description: Relative selection weights by rarity tier.

parameters.name: duplicate_suppression_window
parameters.description: Recent rolls excluded or down-weighted to reduce repetition.

parameters.name: pity_threshold_rolls
parameters.description: Roll count after which a protected reward tier is forced or boosted.

parameters.name: player_level_gate_delta
parameters.description: Allowed reward level offset from current player or area level.

edge_cases: All entries can become ineligible after filters; the table needs a fallback or explicit no-drop result.
Seeded runs must decide whether loot rolls are reproducible before or after player choices.
Inventory limits, stack caps, and duplicate suppression can reject a selected reward after the roll.
Live balance changes should not corrupt existing saves or deterministic challenge seeds.
common_bugs: Weights are treated as percentages and do not sum to 100 after content edits.
Rarity is rolled twice, once by tier and once by item, making actual odds differ from design intent.
Pity counters reset on failed eligibility checks instead of successful protected drops.
Table edits reorder entries and break seeded generation compatibility.
balancing_notes: Balance expected reward over many rolls, not only the most exciting rare outcome.
Economy sinks, crafting inputs, and upgrade costs should be tuned against actual drop simulations.
accessibility_notes: Avoid relying only on color to communicate rarity; include labels, icons, sound, or sorting.
When randomness affects progression, communicate broad odds or reward pools where appropriate.

implementation_notes.unity: Store tables as ScriptableObjects or imported JSON with validation tests that simulate drop distributions and check ineligible-table fallbacks.
implementation_notes.godot: Represent loot tables as Resources or JSON dictionaries and run editor or CI validation for weights, filters, and missing item IDs.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use DataTables or Primary Data Assets for entries and centralize roll authority on the server for multiplayer reward claims.
implementation_notes.web: Keep loot definitions as data files, use a seeded RNG when reproducibility matters, and log roll context for debugging balance issues.

example_games.title: Diablo
example_games.note: Uses randomized drops as a major item-hunting loop.

example_games.title: Borderlands
example_games.note: Uses loot rarity and random item properties as progression rewards.
